result.markdown: --- title: "Practice Activity 3: Control Flow" subtitle: "Practice with Loops & Conditionals" filters: - versions versions: - instructions: out-dir: ".." yaml: eval: false author: "INSTRUCTIONS" - solution: out-dir: ".." out-file: "pa3-solution.qmd" yaml: author: "SOLUTIONS" eval: true format: html execute: echo: true error: true --- The ba`R`istas at Tidy `R`oasters have received a huge drink order and are preparing to fill it. Your job is to help summarize, and fix, the orders. :::{.version-instructions} ## Part Zero: Accessing the Practice Activity This week there are two files you need to download: 1. Download the template Practice Activity R script here: `pa-3-control-flow.R` 2. Download the coffee order data here: `coffee_orders.xlsx` ::: callout-important Be sure to save both files inside your Week 3 folder of your STAT 1810 folder! ::: ::: ## Part One: Getting the Drink Orders 1. Let's get the drink order data into `R`. Since we haven't covered reading in data yet, we'll make it easy! Follow these steps. 1. In RStudio, go to the *Files* pane and navigate to the saved data. 2. Click on the file name and select *Import Dataset...*. 3. Keep all of the settings and click *Import*. You should now see the `coffee_orders` dataset in your Environment tab (upper right corner). :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} coffee_orders ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 7 Name Type Base Milk Sweetener Temperature Size 1 Miles latte espresso dairy none hot small 2 Connie cortado espresso dairy none hot 3 Jing latte matcha soy vanilla iced medium 4 Bruce coffee coffee none sugar hot large 5 Zoya latte espresso oat lavender hot medium 6 Aja americano espresso none honey iced large 7 Em latte chai soy none hot small 8 Grace coffee coffee dairy none iced medium 9 Gilbert espresso espresso none none hot 10 Maurice latte espresso dairy chocolate iced large 11 Hector latte espresso oat none hot small 12 Alessandra cappuccino espresso soy none hot small 13 Dennis coffee coffee dairy sugar hot medium 14 Dionne latte matcha soy lavender iced large 15 Janae coffee coffee none none hot small ``` ::: ::: ::: ## Part Two: Getting Information About the Drink Orders 2. How many drinks are in the order? Save this number in an object named `num_orders` and print out the value. :::{.version-instructions} ::: {.callout-tip} Hint: Think about what dimension of the data correspond to the number of drinks. What `R` function measures that dimension? ::: ::: :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} num_orders <- nrow(coffee_orders) num_orders ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 15 ``` ::: ::: ::: 3. Think back to last week. What operator allows you to grab a single column from a dataframe? Using that operator, write a line of code that produces a vector of the drink `Base` for all drinks. :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} coffee_orders$Base ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "espresso" "espresso" "matcha" "coffee" "espresso" "espresso" [7] "chai" "coffee" "espresso" "espresso" "espresso" "espresso" [13] "coffee" "matcha" "coffee" ``` ::: ::: ::: 4. Now write a line of code that produces the drink `Base` for the fourth drink order. :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} coffee_orders$Base[4] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "coffee" ``` ::: ::: ::: ## Part Three: Finding Specific Drink Orders 5. Use a `while()` loop to find the first person to order a drink with a chai `Base`. Report this person's full drink order. :::{.version-instructions} ::: {.callout-tip} Hint: Look over the handout to identify components you need for a `while()` loop! ::: ::: :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} base <- "" counter <- 0 while (base != "chai") { counter <- counter + 1 base <- coffee_orders$Base[counter] } coffee_orders[counter,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 7 Name Type Base Milk Sweetener Temperature Size 1 Em latte chai soy none hot small ``` ::: ::: ::: 6. Use a `for()` loop to count the total number of espresso drinks. :::{.version-instructions} ::: {.callout-tip} Hint: You will need to use a conditional statement inside your `for()` loop! ::: ::: :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} num_espresso <- 0 for(i in 1:num_orders){ if (coffee_orders$Base[i] == "espresso") { num_espresso <- num_espresso + 1 } } num_espresso ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ## Part Four: Making Drink Modifications 7. Oh no! Tidy `R`oasters has run out of soy milk. The ba`R`istas decide to use oat milk instead. In the `coffee_orders` dataframe, change all the soy milk requests to oat milk. Use the `if_else()` function from `dplyr` to do this. :::{.version-instructions} ::: {.callout-tip} Hint 1: Remember, you can find the **documentation** for a specific function by running `?FUNCTION_NAME` in the console. Hint 2: Remember to save the updated `Milk` back into the `coffee_orders` dataframe! This is the same step you used in last week's PA! ::: ::: :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} coffee_orders$Milk <- if_else(condition = coffee_orders$Milk == "soy", true = "oat", false = coffee_orders$Milk) ``` ::: ::: 8. The ba`R`istas need to know how much ice to have on hand. For a medium iced drink, they need 4oz of ice. For a large iced drink, they need 6oz of ice. Use a `for()` loop and conditionals to calculate the total ice amount the ba`R`istas need. :::{.version-solution} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ice <- 0 for (i in 1:num_orders) { if (coffee_orders$Temperature[i] == "iced") { if (coffee_orders$Size[i] == "medium") { ice <- ice + 4 } else { ice <- ice + 6 } } } ice ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 26 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::{.version-instructions} ## Canvas Submission You will submit your answers to Questions 2, 5, 6, and 8 on Canvas. :::
